point_001: The proposed method is not compared against any previously published baseline models (e.g., Transformer-XL, Compressive Transformer, kNN-LM, REALM), making it difficult to assess whether the approach offers meaningful improvements over the state of the art.
point_002: No ablation studies are provided to isolate the contribution of individual design choices (e.g., number of kNN layers, choice of layer for memory attachment, value of k).
point_003: Statistical significance is never established: no standard deviation or confidence intervals are reported, making it unclear whether small perplexity differences (e.g., 0.05) are meaningful.
point_004: The paper lacks any analysis of computational cost, training speed, or GPU memory usage, which are critical for evaluating the practical viability of the approach.
point_005: The evaluation is limited to language modeling (perplexity); no downstream tasks such as document-level QA, retrieval, or few-shot/zero-shot benchmarks are included to demonstrate generalizability.
point_006: The PG-19 results are not comparable to prior work (e.g., Compressive Transformer reports 33.6 perplexity vs. ~19 here), likely due to different preprocessing or tokenization, and no effort is made to reconcile or replicate under the same conditions.
point_007: Key implementation details are insufficiently described: how the kNN search query is formed, what exactly is stored and retrieved, the role of keys vs. values in the search, and the rationale for hyperparameter choices (e.g., k=128, memory size M) are not clearly explained or justified.
point_008: Reproducibility is hampered by the absence of released code, lack of hardware/software specifications, and use of web-scraped datasets that may be difficult to reconstruct identically.
point_009: The baseline model uses only 6 layers, which is much smaller than contemporary transformer LMs; it is unclear whether the observed gains would transfer to larger, more competitive models.
point_010: The paper does not discuss or empirically test whether the memory mechanism generalizes beyond 'local' (recent-past) retrieval settings, nor does it compare against a baseline with an equivalent total token budget (local context + memory tokens vs. extended local context alone).
point_011: There is no investigation into whether the model risks over-memorizing training data at the expense of generalization, which is particularly relevant for zero-shot or few-shot transfer scenarios.
point_012: The paper does not explore adaptive or gated triggering of the kNN lookup; the mechanism is applied densely at every position, which may be unnecessarily expensive and could benefit from selective retrieval.
point_013: No qualitative or quantitative evaluation of retrieval quality is provided (e.g., human evaluation or precision/recall of retrieved neighbors), making it hard to understand when and why the memory helps.
